sets.title: Indicative vs Subjunctive
sets.description: Latin uses the subjunctive in many subordinate clause types that use the indicative in English: purpose clauses (ut + subj), result clauses (ut + subj), indirect command (ut + subj), circumstantial cum-clauses, and after verbs of fearing.

pairs.label_a: purpose clause: he came in order to see
pairs.label_b: result clause reading (unusual) or incorrect for purpose
pairs.question: Which expresses that coming was *in order to* see something?
pairs.explanation: Purpose clauses in Latin require ut + subjunctive (videret). «ut vidit» uses the indicative — which is used in certain result or comparative clauses, but not purpose. The subjunctive mood in purpose clauses signals that the seeing is the goal, not a reported fact. This is one of Latin's most consistent subjunctive uses.

pairs.sentence_a: Cum Caesar veniret, milites laeti erant.
pairs.sentence_b: Cum Caesar venit, milites laeti erant.
pairs.question: Which sentence uses the cum-clause to set the broader circumstances around the event?
pairs.explanation: Cum with the imperfect subjunctive (veniret) is the 'circumstantial' or 'causal' cum — it gives the attendant circumstances or reason, often translatable as 'since', 'because', or 'when (in the fuller narrative sense)'. Cum with the indicative (venit) is purely temporal: 'when Caesar came' — a bare time marker. The indicative cum-clause is called 'temporal' or 'inversum'; the subjunctive cum-clause is 'circumstantial'.

sets.title: Ablative Case Uses
sets.description: The Latin ablative is a multifunctional case used for means (instrumental), manner, accompaniment, separation, agent (with passive), cause, time, comparison, and the ablative absolute construction.

pairs.label_a: killed by means of a sword (ablative of means — no preposition)
pairs.label_b: killed by a gladiator (ablative of agent — preposition a/ab required)
pairs.question: Which sentence says the weapon (sword) was used as the means of killing?
pairs.explanation: Ablative of means uses no preposition: «gladio» (by means of a sword). Ablative of agent requires the preposition a/ab: «a gladiatore» (by a gladiator). This is one of Latin's famous distinctions: the instrument that acts on its own requires nothing; the person (agent) who acts requires a/ab. Confusing the two is a reliable error marker.

pairs.label_a: comparative with ablative of comparison (no quam)
pairs.question: Which uses the ablative as a comparison rather than the conjunction quam?
pairs.explanation: Latin comparative adjectives can trigger the ablative of comparison (Gallis = than the Gauls) without the conjunction quam. This ablative of comparison replaces quam + the nominative/accusative of the compared element. Both constructions are classical; the ablative is more concise and literary.

sets.title: Indirect Statement: Accusative + Infinitive
sets.description: Latin uses accusative subject + infinitive (not a conjunction clause) for indirect statement after verbs of saying, thinking, knowing, perceiving.

pairs.question: Which is the standard Classical Latin construction for 'he says that he is coming'?
pairs.explanation: Classical Latin uses accusative + infinitive for indirect statement: eum (accusative subject) + venire (infinitive). This is mandatory in Classical prose (Caesar, Cicero, Livy). The quod-clause construction appears only in late Latin, Vulgate, and Medieval texts. Learning the ACI (accusativus cum infinitivo) is essential for reading classical authors.

sets.title: Gerund vs Gerundive
sets.description: The gerund is a verbal noun (amandi = of loving); the gerundive is a verbal adjective implying obligation (liber legendus = a book to be read / a book that ought to be read). They look similar but function differently.

pairs.label_a: the art of loving (gerund: verbal noun, genitive)
pairs.label_b: a girl to be loved / a loveable girl (gerundive: verbal adjective on the noun)
pairs.question: Which uses the form as a verbal noun rather than a verbal adjective?
pairs.explanation: «amandi» in «ars amandi» is the gerund (genitive case of the verbal noun) — translated 'of loving'. «amanda» in «puella amanda» is the gerundive — a verbal adjective agreeing with puella in gender/number/case, expressing obligation or fitness: 'a girl who ought to be loved' or 'a lovable girl'. The forms look similar but their functions diverge: the gerund is a noun; the gerundive is an adjective.

sets.title: Perfect vs Imperfect Aspect
sets.description: Latin perfect encodes completed action or present state resulting from past action. Latin imperfect encodes ongoing, continuous, or repeated past action.

pairs.label_a: the Romans had conquered Carthage (completed result)
pairs.label_b: the Romans were conquering Carthage (ongoing process)
pairs.question: Which sentence presents the conquest as an ongoing struggle, not a completed result?
pairs.explanation: «vicerant» (pluperfect = perfect stem) presents a completed prior action — the conquest is accomplished. «vincebant» (imperfect) presents conquest as an ongoing, continuous process — the Romans were in the act of conquering. The same aspectual distinction seen in Spanish and Russian: imperfect = camera panning across action; perfect = plot point reached.
